# Questions in Livestream 71

Live Stream #71: The First of Octember

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp. The links use HTML anchors so GitHub can render them with `target="_blank"`.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/u6bP2Ikr9Xs?t=313" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">5:13</a> | Why does the Bible speak about physical endings but not physical origins, and does that make full preterism plausible? | He rejected the premise, arguing that Genesis and eschatology are framed more by purpose and teleology than by bare physical description, and he treated full preterism as outside historic Christian orthodoxy. | He argued that the question assumes too much: Genesis is not mainly explaining physical mechanism but God assigning purpose to the cosmos, and eschatology is not simply the end of physical existence but the fulfillment and reassignment of creation's purpose. On that framing, full preterism does not solve the issue, and he objected especially to its denial of a future bodily return of Christ and resurrection of the dead. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/u6bP2Ikr9Xs?t=634" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">10:34</a> | When is his discussion with Titus Kennedy, and has it already happened? | He said it was still upcoming and described it as a serious discussion on the date of the Exodus. | He said the Titus Kennedy discussion had not happened yet and was scheduled for Wednesday, October 19 at 4 p.m. Pacific on Sean McDowell's channel. He described it as a rare serious exchange between qualified advocates of the early and late Exodus dates, contrasting it with less useful debates and saying the date of the Exodus would be the focused topic. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/u6bP2Ikr9Xs?t=781" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">13:01</a> | Will he bring popcorn to the Titus Kennedy interview? | Probably not. He wanted the exchange to stay serious rather than turn into a spectacle. | He said bringing popcorn would look inflammatory, because he wanted the discussion to test arguments and data rather than become a show. He hoped Kennedy would challenge his late-date view seriously, comparing the value of such a challenge to an oral defense where strong questions reveal whether an argument can hold up. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/u6bP2Ikr9Xs?t=1230" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">20:30</a> | Is Christianity surviving, becoming accepted, and then dominant in Rome prophetically significant, perhaps foreshadowed by Daniel 7? | He said that reading is plausible, especially in light of Daniel 7's holy ones receiving the kingdom, though other scholars may differ. | After reading Daniel 7:18 and 7:26-27, he said the language about the holy ones receiving an everlasting kingdom could plausibly foreshadow Christianity. He connected this to Israelite religion dividing after Jerusalem's destruction into rabbinic Judaism and Christianity, while noting that other scholars might interpret the prophecy differently. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/u6bP2Ikr9Xs?t=1407" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">23:27</a> | Are there similarities between the feasts in Leviticus and Egyptian festivals? | He said Egyptian festival practice is hard to reconstruct and differs from Leviticus in priestly and communal participation, though there are broader ritual similarities such as purity practices. | He said Egyptian festivals are difficult to compare because temple scenes rarely preserve the full order, liturgy, or performance details, even when calendars attest many festivals. He contrasted Egypt's more priestly, top-down festivals with Leviticus, where the whole assembly has required participation, while allowing broader similarities in purity and priestly ritual. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/u6bP2Ikr9Xs?t=1729" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">28:49</a> | How can a futurist handle New Testament time statements that seem to demand a soon coming? | He said `soon` can mean imminent or sudden rather than necessarily within a short calendar interval, which is how he thinks most futurist exegetes handle the issue. | He said the futurist response is not to make `soon` mean later, but to read it as sudden or imminent: when the event comes, it comes quickly and unexpectedly. He warned against a one-meaning fallacy, noting that words can have multiple lexical senses, and said many exegetes understand these statements as immediacy of arrival rather than a guaranteed short time span. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/u6bP2Ikr9Xs?t=1890" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">31:30</a> | What is his opinion on the twelve tribes of Israel, Gilead in Judges 5:17, Simeon, and the claim that the twelve tribes are a myth? | He rejected the myth claim, said Gilead was not an Israelite tribe, and argued that some tribes or groups are included or excluded in lists because of their obligations in particular contexts. | He said he did not regard the twelve tribes as mythical and read Judges 5 as referring to obligations in a particular military context rather than redefining Gilead as a tribe. In his explanation, Gilead was a non-Israelite group with treaty and military obligations, while Simeon and other groups could be omitted from blessing lists when they failed their obligations or when the context called for a different grouping. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/u6bP2Ikr9Xs?t=2306" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">38:26</a> | What is his opinion of the Hebrew Roots movement and Black Hebrew Israelites? | He treated them as very different movements, criticizing Black Hebrew Israelite claims about lineage and race while describing Hebrew Roots as a legalistic movement that tends toward works-based salvation. | He distinguished the two sharply. He criticized Black Hebrew Israelite claims that African Americans are the genetic heirs of biblical Israel and that modern Jews are pretenders, and he criticized Hebrew Roots as Torah-observance legalism that can become works-based salvation and hostility to Paul. He considered both harmful, but for different reasons: racial triumphalism in one case and bondage to law in the other. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/u6bP2Ikr9Xs?t=2828" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">47:08</a> | Does a new clan branching off mean the older clan can still continue? | Yes. He said a parent clade can persist for centuries after a new clade or clan branches from it. | He confirmed the questioner's understanding: a new clan or clade can split off while the parent line continues. In his terminology, the original clan remains a parent clade, and the new clan becomes another clade, so the older line can persist for hundreds of years after the branching event. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/u6bP2Ikr9Xs?t=2935" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">48:55</a> | What does he think about dating Job to the seventh to fourth century BC based on Arabic and Edomite words? | He said Job could plausibly fall in a broad range, roughly from the tenth to fifth century BC, and that Arabic and Edomite vocabulary does not force a late date. | He said he has no problem with Job being written in the seventh or sixth century, but the vocabulary evidence does not require a date that late. He argued that Arabic and Edomite references can be earlier than some reconstructions allow, citing earlier Egyptian attestation for Edom and a broad Iron Age context, so he preferred a wide possible range from roughly the tenth to fifth century BC. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/u6bP2Ikr9Xs?t=3256" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">54:16</a> | Since Genesis mentions Ephron the Hittite, is there evidence for Hittites in Canaan during Abraham's time? | He said direct evidence for Hittites in Abraham's time is lacking, but early Hittite history is shadowy enough that their presence cannot be ruled out. | He said the Old Hittite kingdom is poorly understood and direct evidence for Hittites in Canaan during Abraham's time is lacking. Still, because early Hittite movements and presence are obscure, he did not think the Genesis reference can be dismissed simply because the evidence is thin. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/u6bP2Ikr9Xs?t=3506" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">58:26</a> | Does Deuteronomy as a whole follow the structure of Hittite treaties, even if there were multiple redactions? | He accepted the Hittite-treaty structure but was skeptical of redaction criticism as usually framed, allowing for updated language, spelling, interpretive glosses, and stitched-together sections. | He said Deuteronomy's Hittite treaty structure is well supported and that he is on board with that comparison. His hesitation was with redaction criticism as a model of repeated oral and written reshaping; he instead allowed for updated spellings and verbal forms, interpretive glosses, and sections stitched together in a deliberate pattern. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/u6bP2Ikr9Xs?t=3760" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:02:40</a> | Did the worship of Yahweh have Egyptian origins, and is there evidence for the divine name before the first millennium BC? | He said Yahweh worship does not seem Egyptian in origin and is more likely Midianite, while the Shasu of Yhw inscription may be relevant but is not certain proof. | He rejected an Egyptian origin for Yahweh worship, including attempts to connect Yahweh with an Egyptian moon-god name. He connected the biblical narrative to Moses encountering Yahweh in Midian and said the Shasu of Yhw inscription may point toward an earlier divine name in an Edom-Midian setting, but he stressed that it could also be a place or personal name and should be treated cautiously. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/u6bP2Ikr9Xs?t=4026" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:07:06</a> | What is his opinion on the Yu-Gi-Oh pharaoh question? | The question wording is unclear in the transcript. Once he understood it was about Yu-Gi-Oh, he said he had not watched it yet and could not really answer. | He initially could not understand the question until the Yu-Gi-Oh context was supplied. Once he understood it was an anime question, he said he had not watched Yu-Gi-Oh yet, so he could not answer it responsibly, though he said it was on his watch list and that debate preparation had kept him from getting to it. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/u6bP2Ikr9Xs?t=4262" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:11:02</a> | In Deuteronomy 23:7, were the Hyksos already gone, and were the Egyptians native Egyptians rather than Hyksos? | He said the Hyksos were long gone by then, were a ruling group rather than an ethnicity, and that Deuteronomy's Egyptian reference is not to the Hyksos. | He said the Hyksos had been gone for about 300 years by the time of the Exodus context and were not an ethnicity but foreign rulers over some Amorite Semitic groups in the Delta. Therefore Deuteronomy's reference to Egyptians should be understood as native Egyptians, not Hyksos, with the text distinguishing the Joseph-era rulers from the later native dynasty that enslaved Israel. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/u6bP2Ikr9Xs?t=4535" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:15:35</a> | Is the man with six fingers and toes in 2 Samuel 21:20 a cultural idiom or literal? | He said it was probably literal, but carried a Philistine or Canaanite cultural meaning tied to disruptive magic and supernatural favor. | He read the six fingers and toes as literal rather than an idiom like "two left feet." The cultural point, he said, is that Philistine and Canaanite magical thinking could treat physical disruption from normality as a sign of supernatural power, in contrast to Israelite religious emphasis on ordered normality and purity. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/u6bP2Ikr9Xs?t=4912" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:21:52</a> | Did gnosticism begin after Christianity, or is it an older belief system that used Christianity to allegorize its doctrines? | He said gnosticism seems older than Christianity, arising from Egyptian mystery religion mixed with Platonism and later Jewish and Christian symbols. | He said gnosticism appears to predate Christianity, citing Philo's references to Gnostic-type groups and tracing the movement to Egyptian mystery religion, especially Thoth-Hermes traditions, syncretized with Platonism. In his account it adopted Jewish symbols in the second and first centuries BC and then reused Christian texts and symbols for its own allegorical system. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/u6bP2Ikr9Xs?t=5465" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:31:05</a> | What is his opinion on cousin marriages in the Bible, and are they significant, right, or wrong today? | He said biblical kinship terms are broader than modern cousin language, that cousin marriage may be permitted but is not beneficial, especially because of genetic concerns with close cousin marriages. | He emphasized that biblical kinship terms are looser than modern English categories: a father can be a grandfather, a son can be a grandson, and a "cousin" relationship may be more distant than modern readers assume. He thought cousin marriage may be morally permitted under biblical law, unlike closer incestuous relations, but he would not endorse it today because first-cousin marriages especially can carry serious genetic risks for offspring. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/u6bP2Ikr9Xs?t=5877" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:37:57</a> | Would it have been unusual for Genesis's original audience to say that women were created in the image of God? | No. He said Genesis 1 explicitly includes male and female in the image of God, while Genesis 2 also lets women be understood as related to man. | He said Genesis 1:26-27 makes the point directly by speaking of mankind in God's image and then specifying male and female. He added that Genesis 2 also lets women be seen as related to man because woman is made from man's side, so the original audience could understand women as both image of God and, in another respect, image of man. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/u6bP2Ikr9Xs?t=6111" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:41:51</a> | What does he think of the idea that Christ was based on pagan myth, especially Horus? | He rejected it strongly, saying Horus does not die and rise like Christ and that serious scholars do not support the claimed parallel. | He said the Horus parallel is not supported by relevant scholarship and does not match the gospel narrative. Horus is not killed and resurrected like Christ, does not undergo the same kind of death, and belongs to a very different mythic story involving conflict with Seth and the Egyptian divine family; even Osiris would be a closer comparison, though still not the same. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/u6bP2Ikr9Xs?t=6427" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:47:07</a> | Is Titus Kennedy better than Scott Stripling? | He said they may be different and that both may be good field archaeologists, but the real question is how well Kennedy will do as a historian in the discussion. | He said it was too early to judge Kennedy in the same terms and framed the issue as field archaeology versus historical interpretation. He regarded many ABR-associated people as competent excavators but prone to biased interpretation, so he said the upcoming debate would show how well Kennedy functions as a historian. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/u6bP2Ikr9Xs?t=6571" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:49:31</a> | How complicated were Egyptian lines of succession, and were they like modern monarchies that search the family tree for a senior heir? | He said Egyptian succession was simpler: sons of the great queen came first, then sons of minor queens, then daughters, and after the royal harem was exhausted a succession crisis often produced a new dynasty. | He said Egyptian succession did not work like later monarchies searching widely through a family tree. Priority usually went to sons of the great queen, then sons of minor queens, then daughters; once the royal harem was exhausted, bloodline claims became too numerous and power mattered more, so a vizier, general, or appointed heir could resolve the crisis and begin a new line. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/u6bP2Ikr9Xs?t=7088" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:58:08</a> | If the Hyksos invited Israel into Egypt, does that make Deuteronomy 23:7's reason for not detesting Egyptians invalid? | He said no. Even if the Hyksos invited them, the Israelites still sojourned on Egyptian land after native Egyptians controlled it, so the reason still works. | He said the Hyksos may have invited Jacob's family into Goshen, but the Israelites continued living on and using Egyptian land after native Egyptians retook control. He compared it to a lease that remains after ownership changes: even under a bad new landlord, the tenant is still using that land, so the rationale that Israel had been an alien in Egypt still stands. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/u6bP2Ikr9Xs?t=7598" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:06:38</a> | Has `Allah` always meant `God`, or is it a pagan divine name? | He said it is the generic Arabic Semitic word for God, used even by Arabic Christians, and not the name of a pagan deity. | He said Allah is derived from the same Semitic word family as El and Elohim and functions as the Arabic word for God or a god, including before Islam. He rejected claims that it comes from a pagan deity such as the moon god Sin and noted that Arabic Christians use Allah and phrases such as insha'Allah without invoking a pagan god. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/u6bP2Ikr9Xs?t=7864" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:11:04</a> | What did the cherubim in the temple and on the Ark look like, and were they like Ezekiel's vision or Egyptian in style? | He said they were probably Egyptian in style, likely women with wings for arms, and not like the later cherubim of Ezekiel's vision. | He said the Ark and temple cherubim were probably in an earlier Egyptian or broader ancient Near Eastern style, comparable to women with winged arms, with examples also appearing in Canaanite material such as the Megiddo ivories. Ezekiel's multi-winged and multi-headed imagery reflects later iconographic developments, not necessarily the form used on the Ark. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/u6bP2Ikr9Xs?t=8089" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:14:49</a> | What is his opinion of the deuterocanon / apocrypha? | He said those books can provide useful historical context and literary value, but he does not regard them as Scripture. | He said the deuterocanonical and apocryphal books can be interesting, historically valuable, and sometimes artistically strong, mentioning Maccabees and Tobit as examples of useful reading. But he does not treat them as inspired Scripture, preferring the narrower canon used in Protestant contexts, while acknowledging that other Christian traditions receive a broader canon. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/u6bP2Ikr9Xs?t=8352" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:19:12</a> | When did gnosticism become entangled with Christianity? | He placed the major entanglement in the second century AD, when Gnostic gospels and strong Christian responses to them become visible. | He said the entanglement happened very quickly, especially in the second century AD. By the late second century Gnostic gospels and church-father responses such as Irenaeus are visible, while earlier second-century writers such as Clement of Alexandria already show Christianity having to respond to Gnostic claims and reinterpretations. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/u6bP2Ikr9Xs?t=8654" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:24:14</a> | What is his take on Wisdom 2:10-20 as Jesus prophecy, and could the deuterocanon be sub-inspired? | He said he would need to look again at Wisdom, but rejected the idea of sub-inspiration: a text is either inspired or not, though it can still be useful. | He said he would need to reread Wisdom before judging the proposed prophecy, but he rejected "sub-inspired" as a category. In his view, inspiration means God prepares the writer so the text is God-breathed and usable for teaching, correction, reproof, and rebuke; apocryphal texts may still be useful literature and historical context, but that is different from being inspired Scripture. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/u6bP2Ikr9Xs?t=9005" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:30:05</a> | Is Moses's use of water in Genesis 1 a reference to deity? | He said water can symbolize divine realities elsewhere, but he did not think Genesis 1 requires that reading; there it is more about creation being ordered and given purpose. | He acknowledged that water can carry theological symbolism in passages such as John 3, but warned against forcing the same symbol to mean the same thing everywhere. In Genesis 1, he said the stronger point is that God takes unassigned space and gives it place, purpose, function, and order: waters are separated, sea and sky receive their inhabitants, and creation becomes an ordered temple-like world. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/u6bP2Ikr9Xs?t=9547" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:39:07</a> | Were the gnostics persecuted alongside Christians, and why would they want to be tied to an illegal religion? | He said the Romans generally did not target gnostics like Christians because gnostics could accommodate Roman religious expectations and were not bound to Christian confession in the same way. | He said gnostics were not generally targeted by Rome in the same way because they could burn incense, say Caesar is Lord, and adapt to Roman religious expectations without violating their system. Christians were constrained by the confession that Jesus is Lord, while gnostics had fewer fixed doctrinal commitments, accepted many divine beings, and were largely compatible with Roman polytheistic assumptions. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/u6bP2Ikr9Xs?t=9856" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:44:16</a> | Is there a book he recommends on biblical numerology? | No. He affirmed that some biblical numbers are symbolically important, especially `7`, `12`, and `40`, but said most numerology books overextend the evidence. | He did not recommend a book because he thinks biblical numerology is real but limited. Numbers such as 7, 12, and 40 have widely recognized symbolic force, and maybe a few others, but he said books on the subject often need more material than sound exegesis can provide and therefore invent meanings for many numbers beyond what the text supports. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/u6bP2Ikr9Xs?t=10130" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:48:50</a> | Was Moses really 120 when he died, or are the sets of 40 used to mark stages of his life? | He favored the symbolic reading, saying 40 is a common idiom for an unspecified long time and that Moses was probably not literally 120 in this reconstruction. | He said the sets of 40 are likely symbolic stages rather than exact chronology, because 40 is a common biblical and Levantine idiom for an unspecified long period. He thought Moses may have been in his fifties at the Exodus and perhaps in his eighties or nineties at death, while allowing that living to 120 is possible in general but unlikely to be the point here. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/u6bP2Ikr9Xs?t=10423" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:53:43</a> | If the Pentateuch has references written from the perspective of someone in Canaan, does that mean Moses could not be the author? | He said neither extreme is right: Moses did not write every word, but later editorial interpolation and glosses do not mean zero Mosaic authorship. | He said references such as Genesis 36:31 are best explained as later editorial interpolation, not proof that Moses wrote nothing. His middle position is that Moses wrote many smaller documents, later editors stitched them together, updated language and spelling, and added linking notes or glosses from a later Canaan perspective without erasing Mosaic authorship altogether. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/u6bP2Ikr9Xs?t=11371" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:09:31</a> | What does he think of Paul Copan's books on God and Old Testament moral problems? | He had not read the books, but said Copan's speaking on the subject sounded mostly sound and thorough, though he disagreed with some solutions such as the minority view on Jephthah's daughter. | He said he had not read Copan's books but had heard him speak and found most of what he heard sound and thorough, with heavy engagement with scholarship. His main stated disagreement concerned Jephthah's daughter: he rejected the minority view that she was only dedicated to a nunnery-like life, arguing that the Hebrew term points to a burnt offering and that her mourning concerns lost posterity before death. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/u6bP2Ikr9Xs?t=11844" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:17:24</a> | Bach versus Beethoven: who would he put his money on? | Jokingly and musically, he picked Bach, saying Bach adapts well to a single instrument while Beethoven usually needs a large orchestra to shine. | He first joked as if the question were about a fight, choosing Bach, then answered musically that it depends on the setting. Beethoven is magnificent with a full orchestra, but Bach adapts more easily to a single instrument or small arrangement, and because Falk personally plays and enjoys Bach more, he chose Bach both jokingly and musically. |
